serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Emperor Herbal Chicken is approximately 200g, comprising a portion of chicken with herbal broth, as commonly served in a Singaporean Chinese restaurant or home setting.

nutrient_source.title: Emperor Herbal Chicken — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutritional values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Emperor Herbal Chicken (cr_id F11020027). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked chicken with skin (USDA SR Legacy #05064) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this dish; value of 1.5g/100g is used as a defensible estimate consistent with the fat profile of skin-on braised chicken.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate braised meat dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Emperor Herbal Chicken. GI is estimated at 35 (low) based on the analogous category of low-carbohydrate braised meat dishes with herbal broths, consistent with University of Sydney GI Database entries for similar protein-dominant, low-carbohydrate cooked meat preparations. Glycemic load per 200g serving = (35 × 1.97 × 2) / 100 = 1.4, which is negligible. The very low carbohydrate content (1.97g/100g) means GI classification has minimal practical clinical significance.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 284mg/100g — borderline; partially offset by estimated potassium ~220mg/100g from herbal ingredients
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should consume Emperor Herbal Chicken in moderate portions (≤150g per meal), request reduced soy sauce during preparation, and avoid adding extra salt, as the 284mg/100g sodium content is in the borderline range despite partial potassium offset from goji berries and red dates.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (1.97g/100g) and sugar (1.41g/100g) with high protein (15.44g/100g) supporting stable postprandial glycaemia
type2_diabetes.guidance: Emperor Herbal Chicken is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content (1.97g/100g) and high protein (15.44g/100g), which together support stable blood glucose levels; however, the natural sugars from goji berries and red dates in the broth should be consumed in moderation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat (5.99g/100g) with estimated low saturated fat (~1.5g/100g); anti-inflammatory herbal components including ginseng, goji berries, and dang gui
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Emperor Herbal Chicken presents a low cardiovascular risk profile with moderate total fat (5.99g/100g), estimated low saturated fat (~1.5g/100g), and polyphenol-rich herbal ingredients that may confer anti-inflammatory and antioxidant benefits; sodium intake should be monitored and soy sauce minimised for individuals with established cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory gingerols, allicin, and polyphenols from Chinese herbs; low saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Emperor Herbal Chicken may benefit individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to anti-inflammatory compounds in ginger, garlic, and Chinese herbs (dang gui, goji berries); however, patients on RA medications such as methotrexate should consult their healthcare provider before regular consumption of medicinal herbal preparations.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Emperor Herbal Chicken (皇帝药材鸡, Huáng Dì Yào Cái Jī) is a traditional Singaporean Chinese dish in which a whole chicken is slow-cooked or braised in a rich medicinal herbal broth. The broth typically contains ginseng, wolfberries (goji berries), red dates (jujube), dang gui (Chinese angelica root), ginger, garlic, soy sauce, and Chinese rice wine. The dish is deeply rooted in Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) philosophy, where the herbal ingredients are believed to tonify qi, nourish blood, and strengthen the body. The chicken becomes tender and infused with the earthy, slightly sweet, and aromatic flavours of the herbs. Nutritionally, Emperor Herbal Chicken is a high-protein (15.44g/100g), moderate-fat (5.99g/100g), very low-carbohydrate (1.97g/100g) dish with 123 kcal per 100g. The dietary fibre content (1.36g/100g) is contributed by the herbal ingredients. Sodium is 284mg/100g, placing it in the borderline range for hypertension management. The dish is clinically suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible glycaemic impact, and presents a low cardiovascular risk profile given its low saturated fat content and anti-inflammatory herbal base. It is commonly served as a main course at family meals, festive occasions, and in traditional Chinese restaurants and zi char stalls across Singapore. The dish is halal-certified in many establishments and is popular across ethnic communities in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Core nutritional values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11020027) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked chicken data as a defensible secondary source. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database category for low-carbohydrate braised meat dishes. All other fields are grounded in approved sources or documented as -1 where data is unavailable.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.5g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked chicken with skin (#05064) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this dish. 2. Potassium (220mg/100g) is an estimate based on the herbal ingredient profile and USDA data for cooked chicken; this value is not directly reported in the HPB entry. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid breakdown for this traditional herbal chicken preparation is not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 4. GI value of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Emperor Herbal Chicken was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. The very low carbohydrate content (1.97g/100g) renders the GI classification of minimal clinical significance. 5. The input record listed gi_value as 0 with confidence 0.477 from an 'Embedded GI Database' — this is not an approved source and has been replaced with a defensible analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework.
